did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning architectures (VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and DenseNet121) for the automated diagnosis and classification of skin cancer (specifically Basal Cell Carcinoma) from dermoscopic images. The study implemented these models for feature extraction and binary classification, achieving an accuracy of 99% with DenseNet121.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is a comparative study presenting original experimental results. It details the implementation of specific deep learning architectures (VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, DenseNet121) on a dataset of 1,000 dermoscopic images, describes the methodology (preprocessing, training, validation), and reports new quantitative performance metrics (e.g., 99% accuracy for DenseNet121). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICITEICS), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Deep Learning Based Skin Cancer Diagnosis and Classification: A Comparative Study
journal: 2025 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Information Technology, Electronics and Intelligent Communication Systems (ICITEICS)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the effectiveness of deep learning architectures, specifically VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, and DenseNet121, for the automated diagnosis and classification of basal cell carcinoma using dermoscopic images. The research utilized a dataset of 1,000 images representing benign and malignant lesions, which underwent extensive preprocessing including scaling, denoising, and segmentation. The models were implemented as both standalone classifiers and feature extractors, with performance assessed through metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score on a split training, validation, and testing set. Comparative results demonstrated that the DenseNet121 model achieved superior performance, recording an accuracy of 99.4% and an F1-score of 0.99. The findings indicate that DenseNet121 outperforms other evaluated deep networks in distinguishing between healthy and cancerous skin tissues, suggesting its potential for reliable clinical support in early skin cancer detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task. The study focuses on distinguishing BCC from healthy skin. The dataset contained 500 cancerous images (labeled as 'Cancer' in Table I) representing BCC.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 500 non-cancerous images (labeled as 'Non-Cancer' in Table I) representing healthy or benign skin tissue.
Mentioned in the abstract as a type of skin cancer, but not included in the experimental dataset or classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label (Cancer vs. Non-Cancer / Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Healthy Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and healthy/non-cancerous skin using dermoscopic images. The models (VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, DenseNet121) output probabilities via a sigmoid activation function for binary cross-entropy loss, with performance evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score on a dataset of 1,000 images (500 cancerous, 500 non-cancerous).

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of 1,000 dermoscopic images used for the study, consisting of benign and malignant lesions. The images were resized to 150x150x3 pixels and normalized. The specific source or public repository name is not explicitly stated in the text, though reference [11] points to a Kaggle skin disease dataset which may have been the origin, but the paper treats it as a specific collection for this study.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet, DenseNet121) with data augmentation (rotations, zooms, flips) to improve flexibility.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor model performance and generalization during training (e.g., tracking validation loss and accuracy curves).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation, including calculation of confusion matrices, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score.
use.other: Pre-trained models were initialized with weights from ImageNet, but the primary dataset for the specific task was the custom 1,000-image collection.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Curve (AUC) / ROC value

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report performance metrics including True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), False Negatives (FN), Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Negative Predictive Value (NPV), and F1-score for each model.
To evaluate the balance between sensitivity and specificity and to determine the Area Under the Curve (AUC) value for model discrimination capability.

statistical_methods_used.method: Training and Validation Loss/Accuracy curve monitoring
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To track model convergence, generalization, and potential overfitting during the 50 epochs of training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and ROC curves.

DenseNet121.notes: Top-performing model. Training loss decreased steadily. Validation loss decreased steadily. Accuracy curve showed training accuracy leveling out and validation accuracy reaching high levels (specific final validation accuracy value not explicitly stated as a single number in text, but ROC is 1.00).

VGG19.notes: Training accuracy leveled out at 0.92. Validation accuracy reached 0.95. Training loss decreased steadily. Validation loss went down but fluctuated.

InceptionV3.notes: No specific ROC, loss, or accuracy curve values reported in text for this model.

MobileNet.notes: No specific ROC, loss, or accuracy curve values reported in text for this model.

Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset (1,000.notes: DenseNet121 achieved the highest accuracy (0.994) and F1-score (0.99). The paper states DenseNet121 is better than other networks. VGG19 had the lowest accuracy among the four (0.956).

architecture_details.implementation_notes: Models were implemented as both standalone classifiers and feature extractors. Layers were locked to prevent overfitting. DenseNet121 utilizes depth-wise separable convolutions for feature extraction.

classification_or_output_head.description: A big layer for binary classification was added to the pre-trained models.

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel normalization to a range between 0 and 1
Label encoding for class numerical values
Data augmentation including rotations, zooms, and flips

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training dataset to increase model flexibility and add variety. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to the training dataset to increase model flexibility and add variety. Specific zoom ranges or probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: DenseNet121 achieved superior performance compared to VGG19, InceptionV3, and MobileNet, recording an accuracy of 99.4% (reported as 99% in the abstract) and an F1-score of 0.99.

The DenseNet121 model demonstrated excellent generalization capabilities, with steadily decreasing training and validation loss curves over 50 epochs, indicating effective feature capture without overfitting.
DenseNet121 achieved a ROC value of 1.00, indicating perfect discrimination between healthy and cancerous tissues in the test set.
The study confirms that deep learning architectures, particularly DenseNet121, are effective for the automated binary classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) from dermoscopic images.

The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the dataset size (1,000 images) and suggest future research should evaluate model performance on larger, more diverse datasets and in real-time clinical applications.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. Healthy Skin) and does not evaluate other critical skin cancer types such as melanoma, seborrheic keratosis, or benign nevi, despite mentioning them in the abstract.

The dataset is small (1,000 images total) and sourced from a single custom collection (referenced as a Kaggle dataset), raising concerns about population bias, demographic diversity, and generalizability to broader clinical populations.
The dataset split is highly imbalanced for robust evaluation, with only 100 images (50 per class) allocated to the testing set, which may lead to unstable performance estimates.

The paper reports perfect discrimination metrics for the top model (DenseNet121), including an ROC-AUC of 1.00 and 100% specificity, which is unusually high for medical imaging studies and may indicate overfitting or data leakage, although the authors claim effective generalization.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between the compared models.
The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets, limiting the evidence for clinical deployment and real-world applicability.

The paper does not report computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs, parameter count) which are crucial for assessing the feasibility of deploying these models in resource-constrained clinical settings.
